evidence_quotes.quote: prevent publishers from removing access from videogames that the user purchased or purchased an in-game purchase in by making them unplayable.
a problematic feature of many video games is their ability to become completely unplayable when the publisher stops running the server that is required for them to be played.

paragraphs.text: I would like to suggest adding consumer protections that would prevent publishers from removing access from videogames that the user purchased or purchased an in-game purchase in by making them unplayable. In the document the call for evidence mentions: addressing problematic features of digital products such as in video games, I think a problematic feature of many video games is their ability to become completely unplayable when the publisher stops running the server that is required for them to be played. When this happens they usually give you a notice in advance, rarely a refund. It is quite unlikely for a videogame to remain playable after official support ends, even though it staying playable should be legally required. As of 8th October 2025 the Dead games list hosted at https://stopkillinggames.wiki.gg/wiki/Dead_game_list out of the 686 games, that are not officially supported anymore only 37 or 5.39% remained playable because of the games developers, 225 or 32.80% of them survived by being repaired unofficially by the games fans, and 424 or 61.81% of them are dead. Without any legal requirement for games that were purchased or which a user bought in-game purchases for them becoming unplayable is common and it will likely continue being that way. Just this is very bad, but thats not everything: many games, which can currently be bought or contain in-game purchases are going to die eventually and most of them are likely going to become completely unplayable. The aforementioned Dead games list contains 402 games that may become completely unplayable. And this is just an approximation of the current state of these videogames there are likely more and there will likely be more of them in the future. Even when considering videogames purely as a product, this should still be illegal, as its effectively taking away a purchase. But I also consider videogames art, and art shouldnt be lost; it should be preserved as the important part of our culture and humanity that it is. I personally currently play a videogame that is listed on the Dead games list as at-risk Hunt: Showdown 1896. Its a pretty good videogame, that is quite unique, influential and more, that I purchased and which I would not want to become unplayable. There is also a game Tom Clancys Ghost Recon Phantoms that I played, a long time ago, that is now unplayable, and that I wont get to play again, which is sad, because it was interesting and I wouldve liked to revisit it, but I cant. Beyond the questionable legality and cultural loss there is also the aspect of waste monetary, personal, professional and even material. The time and money the user has spent on the videogame is lost. The people who worked on the videogame cannot play it or effectively refer to the work theyve done, as anyone who hasnt played the game before it was made unplayable cannot play it anymore. If the videogame was sold on a physical medium like an optical disc and the case that the medium came with also become waste, as the program that is on it no longer works. I hope my comment has sufficiently covered and explained why this problem is relevant to the Digital Fairness Act and why it should be considered for inclusion.
